ProjectDescription: The NPO is a nonprofit organization providing fast, effective mental health assistance for individuals in crisis with dignity and respect. Users submit structured requests, which are triaged and prioritized by an AI Prioritization Agent to flag urgent cases. Trained volunteers access a Volunteer Management Dashboard to respond appropriately based on age, gender, and type of distress. Integrated geospatial tools from the Geospatial Support via Cloud Mapping API locate nearby mental health resources, while automated reports from the Reporting and Analytics module track trends and performance. RapidMind ensures timely, sensitive, and targeted support to prevent escalation.

Lists.Name: Sprint 8
Lists.Description:
This is the final stabilization push. Your mission is to switch from feature building to a collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team to ensure the platform is production-ready. 
 
 You are moving from the "feature" phase to the "quality" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying glitches and ensuring the user experience is flawless before the final handoff. Since everyone is doing QA, you will monitor the Meeting Room for every "Bugs case" logged and address them in your dedicated branch. 
 
 Success means a pixel-perfect, error-free frontend that has been fully merged into the main repository.

Cards.Name: Infrastructure Audit & High-Level Data Modeling
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 This week is your "Technical Landing": a low-pressure space to explore the project's foundations. 
 
 Your objective is to partner with the PM to translate their business journey into a technical blueprint. 
 
 You will verify the environment and create the "Big Picture" database model that will serve as our project's technical map.
ChecklistItems: Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Project Kickoff" to meet your teammates and understand the project’s high-level mission.
Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical exploration.
Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.
Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE is perfectly synced with the repository.

GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
Infrastructure Audit: Connect to the Postgres DB and perform a "Stress Test" (e.g., creating and deleting a dummy table) to ensure the environment is stable for future data.

Collaborative Journey & Persona Review: Sit down with the PM to review their Global Journey Diagram and review the UI/UX Designer's new User Personas. Use this session to brainstorm the core entities and relationships (e.g., Individuals in Crisis, Volunteers, Coordinators) needed to support the entire system.
High-Level Schema Diagram: Based on your review with the PM, create a visual ERD (Entity Relationship Diagram) showing the high-level data architecture.
Blueprint Centralization: Save your schema diagram in the Meeting Room so the team can reference our "Data Source of Truth." (Your Mentor can guide you on the process).

Cards.Name: Frontend Orientation & Environmental Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the front line! 
 
 We are thrilled to have you on board. 
 
 This first week is your "Technical Landing" - a low-pressure space to explore your new digital playground. 
 
 While the PM and UI/UX teams define the project's soul, your objective is to ensure your development environment is perfectly tuned and to collaborate on the project's global structure. 
 
 You are the team's bridge between design and code.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and align on our high-level mission.
[ ] Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical setup.
[ ] Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.

[ ] Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE and build tools are perfectly synced with the repository.

[ ] GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
[ ] Tech Stack Audit: Explore the project's frontend boilerplate (React/Next.js/etc.) and verify that all necessary dependencies and linting rules are correctly installed.
[ ] Collaborative Journey Review: Sit down with the PM and the Backend Developer to review the Global Journey Diagram. Use this to identify potential UI complexities or navigation challenges early.

[ ] Global Styling Foundation: Based on the UI/UX Designer's initial research, begin initializing the project's "Foundations"—set up the global CSS variables for the color palette, typography scales, and spacing units.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem & CRM Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team!

We are so excited to have you as our "Market Intelligence" lead.

This first week is a low-pressure opportunity for you to learn about the mental health support landscape we are entering.

Your mission is to map out the "playing field" - the ecosystem of crisis support services - and identify who the key players are.

Think of the AI Mentor as your research consultant who will hold your hand while you learn our internal coordination module.

This week is all about discovery and building a solid knowledge base for the team.
ChecklistItems: Team Kickoff: Join the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and understand our shared starting point.
Anchor Alignment: Start an introductory conversation with the AI Customer to understand the business world they operate in every day.
CRM & Research Setup: Ask the AI Mentor for a step-by-step walkthrough of the CRM module and for professional guidance on creating a "Stakeholder Map."

Industry Discovery: Conduct "Desk Research" on the mental health crisis support landscape to understand how it works (e.g., how individuals in distress receive timely help from support services or trained responders).
Competitive Analysis: Review 2-3 existing global solutions and summarize how they approach this industry.
Stakeholder Mapping: Identify the key groups involved (individuals in crisis, volunteers, coordinators, and mental health service providers) and input these entities into your CRM screen.
NSM Prep: Get familiar with the term NSM (Next Sprint Meeting); you’ll share your ecosystem findings here to help the team stay grounded in the industry's reality.

Cards.Name: Vision, Discovery & The PRD Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the helm! 
 
 Your mission this week is to transform initial ambiguity into a solid strategic roadmap. 
 
 You are the Strategic Detective, digging deep into the "Why" and "Who" of this project. 
 
 The Big Picture: While we are establishing the foundations now, keep in mind that the actual construction of our first module begins in Sprint 3. 
 
 This week is about building the trust and the blueprint: the Project PRD - required to make that build successful.
ChecklistItems: Project Kickoff: Lead the official "Project Kickoff" in the meeting room. Set the vision, align the team on the mission, and facilitate introductions.
Kickoff Synthesis: Publish a concise meeting summary and a list of immediate action items in the Team Chat to ensure Day 1 alignment.
Relationship Building: Initiate introductory conversations with each teammate (BE, FE, UI/UX, BizDev) to establish a personal connection and a supportive team culture.
Team Connection Recap: Post a high-level summary of the team’s collective strengths and support needs in the Team Chat to reinforce the collaborative foundation.
PRD Initialization: Initialize the Product Requirements Document (PRD). This is the central home for the project’s DNA. Use it to document every strategic decision you make this week.

Problem Validation: Interview the AI Customer to confirm reality vs. assumptions regarding their current workflow. Document their "Pain Points" and "Success Criteria" in the Discovery section of your PRD.
Customer Insight Broadcast: Share the key takeaways and prioritized action items from the customer interview in the Team Chat to ground the team in the user's reality.
North Star & KPIs: Define the project's North Star Metric and 3–5 supporting KPIs. Add these to the PRD to serve as the decision filter for the entire journey.

Actor Alignment: Identify the main actors (e.g., Individuals in Crisis, Volunteers, Coordinators, Admins). Map out their primary responsibilities and "Needs" within the User Persona section of the PRD.
Journey Mapping & Centralization: Create a visual diagram of the end-to-end mental health support journey. Integrate this diagram directly into the PRD so it serves as the project’s visual anchor.
PRD Accessibility: Save the PRD in the Team Room. Ensure it is available to everyone and clearly marked as the project's "Source of Truth."
Team Journey Demonstration: Set a meeting with the entire team to demonstrate the Journey Map and walk through the Draft PRD. Gather feedback and ensure everyone sees the "Big Picture."
Vision Alignment Summary: Publish the final meeting summary and a consolidated list of feedback-driven action items in the Team Chat to officially close the loop on Sprint 1.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem Discovery & Research Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 As our lead designer, you are the architect of the user's experience. 
 
 This first week is your "Discovery" phase: a low-pressure space to understand the industry and define who we are building for. 
 
 Before diving into the visuals, you will coordinate your research roadmap with the Product Manager. 
 
 Your goal is to gather the competitive intelligence and persona insights needed to shape a high-impact design strategy in the weeks ahead.
ChecklistItems: Project Kickoff: Participate in the Project Kickoff meeting to align on the mission and meet your new teammates.
Product Manager Alignment: Meet with the Product Manager to plan your specific research tasks and milestones for the remainder of the sprint.
Figma Setup: Initialize your Figma project and link it to the Meeting Room. Ask the AI Mentor if you need instructions on how to sync the files.

Market Research: Analyze existing solutions in related fields (e.g., crisis helplines, volunteer coordination platforms, or digital mental health support tools). Note the pros and cons of each and upload a summary to the Meeting Room.
Visual Inspiration Board: Create a Mood Board or "Inspiration Table" in Figma. Focus on UI patterns, color psychology, and layouts that align with the project goals.

User Personas: Define initial User Personas (e.g., individual in crisis, volunteer responder, coordinator). Document their specific needs, motivations, and potential pain points. Add persona designs to the project's Figma.
User Journey Insights: Review the Global User Journey created by the Product Manager. Flag any specific areas or flows that require extra UX attention.

Cards.Name: Data Architecture & Technical Readiness
Cards.Description:
This week is about definition over execution.

Your goal is to move from the abstract "Big Picture" to a precise technical blueprint for the Crisis Intake Experience.

You are the architect of the data flow.

By the end of this week, you will have established the plan for how the Frontend and Backend communicate.

You aren't building the final logic yet; you are preparing the "manual" so that the team can build with total confidence in Sprint 3.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.

Tooling and Library Audit: Review your current backend stack and libraries (e.g., validation libraries, database drivers). Ensure everything is optimized and ready for the specific data-entry and form-handling tasks coming next week.

Frontend Data Sync (General Handshake): Meet with the Frontend Developer to agree on the general "Blueprint" for how data will move between the UI and the server (e.g., Auth patterns, error handling, and endpoint structure).

Requirements Sync (Tables and Fields): Once the PM has finalized the User Story, meet to identify the specific Tables and Fields required to support the Crisis Intake Form. Map out exactly what data points need to be stored (e.g., Age, Gender, Location, Short/Detailed Description etc.).

Database Schema Blueprint: Update the Database Schema diagram based on the PM's finalized fields. This acts as the "Contract" for the project's data structure and serves as the team's single source of truth.
Mock Data Provision: Generate a set of "dummy" crisis intake data. The goal is to give the Frontend Developer realistic data to work with in Sprint 3 before you have built the actual database logic.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your technical blueprint. Confirm that your architecture can support the validation rules required by the customer.

Blueprint Delivery (PR and Merge): Commit your final schema designs and mock data files to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to lock in the technical blueprint for the build.

Cards.Name: UI Architecture & Component Scaffolding
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the "Architect" of the interface.

Before we start coding the actual logic for the Crisis Intake Experience in Sprint 3, you will build the reusable "Atoms" that make development fast and consistent.

You are building the "lego pieces" and the "hallways" of the application.

By the end of the week, the team should be able to click through the basic navigation of the flow, even if the forms don't "save" data yet.

This is where we build the skeleton so that next week, we can add the muscle.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.
Component Library Initialization: Set up a dedicated workspace for your reusable UI components (e.g., a /components library folder or a Storybook setup).

Atomic Component Build: Build the foundational, "logic-less" components required for the Crisis Intake Form: Buttons, Input Fields (Text/Select), Loaders, and Success Modals. Apply the Visual Language (Colors and Typography) established by the Designer this week.

Frontend Data Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer to agree on the "Data Blueprint." Confirm the specific "Request" (what the form sends) and "Response" (what the server says back) so that your components are ready for real data.

App Navigation "Hallways": Set up the empty page containers and the URL paths for the Crisis Intake flow (e.g., /submit for the form and /success for the confirmation). This ensures the structural journey is ready for the logic in Sprint 3.
UI/UX Handshake: Review the Designer’s final design. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the layout aligns with the UI components you are preparing.
Milestone: Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your component library and technical readiness. Show the team the "UI Atoms" and the clickable "Hallways" you’ve built.

UI Delivery (PR & Merge): Commit your foundational components and navigation routes to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel. This locks in the UI architecture before the build phase begins.

Cards.Name: Crisis Intake Module
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse. 
 
 Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the module’s technical evolution, and External Validation to ground that progress in user reality. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the Crisis Intake Module is not just a technical achievement, but a user-ready solution. 
 
 You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual operational habits of individuals seeking mental health support.
ChecklistItems:
User Data Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding user data.
Focus: Clarify operational details that will affect the build (e.g., “Do individuals in crisis reliably provide location and contact information, or are some fields often incomplete?”).

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design, meet to conduct a deep-dive. 
 Goal: Ensure you can articulate exactly how the intake logic and validations are intended to work before you head into the field.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential users (e.g., counselors, or social workers). 
 Validation: Present the high-level concept of the intake module to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital workflow (specifically the structured input fields and mobile entry) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of users in distress during a crisis.

Database Intelligence Logging: Use the database management system to document all field insights and stakeholder profiles. 
 Data Management: Ensure you are managing the user data (entering/editing) so the entire team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding user needs.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant mental health research, case studies, or user experience examples. 
 Benchmark: Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. 
 Prioritization: Help the team prioritize input fields and validation logic based on real-world urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 4 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the Crisis Intake Module validation, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Crisis Intake Planning & Design Alignment
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the high-level journey and the actual build.

Your mission is to translate the vision from Sprint 1 into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Crisis Intake Form.

Sprint 2 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 3.

You are the team's Unlocker.

We are focusing entirely on the "Input Foundation" - capturing the needs of individuals seeking mental health support, including age, gender, emotional or psychological issues, and geographic location.

Success means a user can seamlessly enter their details, describe their situation, and indicate urgency via a mobile-optimized form.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the "Crisis Intake Experience" ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 2 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week's setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required Intake Form fields (limit to max 6) and identify the specific IF/THEN validation rules, such as rules for urgency indicators or required fields.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Crisis Intake Module card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name, Field Type (Text, Number, etc.), and whether it is Required (Yes/No). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions, for example, "Success message appears" or "Mandatory field errors trigger correctly".

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the Crisis Intake Form Design and success screens. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Team Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Intake Form logic based on your Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding 
 (4) Assign owners
Sprint 3 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Crisis Intake Module Design
Cards.Description:
This week, you translate research into reality.

Your mission is to move from the mood boards and personas of Sprint 1 directly into a high-fidelity Visual Language that establishes trust and clarity.

You are the Guardian of the Brand. By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the final color palette, typography, and polished UI layouts for the Crisis Intake Form.

Your goal is to ensure the interface doesn't just work, it feels professional, reliable, and accessible from the very first screen.
ChecklistItems: Logo & Visual Language: Interview the AI Customer regarding the product name, logo guidelines, and overall visual direction. Design the product logo and establish the visual language in Figma (e.g., Color Palette and Typography).
Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify specific user needs for the Crisis Intake Form and verify any functional expectations.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Crisis Intake user story is drafted. Review the required data fields and validation logic to ensure your design supports all business and technical constraints.
Intake Form Design: Create a mobile-first design of the intake form in Figma. Focus on layout hierarchy and ease of use on smaller screens to ensure a smooth user experience during crisis situations.

States & Notifications: Design success and error states for all form fields and add them to the Figma design system. Create the corresponding success and error notifications for the post-submission flow.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that your UI components align with the project's component library.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final design to the team. This will serve as the "North Star" for the upcoming development in Sprint 3.
Sprint 4 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the next phase of the Volunteer Dashboard. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Crisis Intake Logic & Data Handling
Cards.Description:
This is it. The Build Week. 
 
 You are no longer just an architect. You are the Intake Specialist. 
 
 Your mission is to build the framework that allows individuals to submit their critical information successfully. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, when a user clicks "Submit" on the frontend, your backend must capture that data, validate it against the established criteria, and store it securely in the database. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the data "Intake Foundation" is secure, accurate, and ready for the next stage of the project.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.
Database Migration: Implement the physical tables in the database based on the schema you finalized in Sprint 2. Ensure all fields (IDs, timestamps, and data types) are correctly configured.
Validation Engine Build: Code the IF/THEN logic as defined in the PM's User Story (e.g., ensuring age is a valid number or urgency indicators are appropriately flagged).

API Endpoint Development: Build the POST endpoint that will receive the intake data. Ensure the response body includes all necessary parameters for UI responsiveness (e.g., specific error codes, unique request IDs, or updated state objects).
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your validation logic and database service. Ensure that "bad data" is rejected with the correct error codes and "good data" is prepared for storage.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from last week to test the endpoint. Confirm that valid data is saved and invalid data triggers the correct error response codes before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the intake form. Verify that their UI components are successfully sending the data to your endpoint and receiving the expected response parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Volunteer Dashboard User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Volunteer Dashboard schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Volunteer Dashboard module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate that the intake data is being successfully captured and stored.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Intake Foundation.

Cards.Name: Crisis Intake Logic & Data Integration
Cards.Description:
This is the Build Week. 
 
 You are no longer just building components. You are building the Experience. 
 
 Your mission is to take the "Atoms" from Sprint 2 and wire them into a fully functional, responsive intake form that captures critical user information and facilitates data storage. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, a user should be able to fill out the intake form, see real-time validation, and receive a clear confirmation or error message upon submission. 
 
 You are responsible for the "Handshake" between the user and the data storage system.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the intake form behavior matches the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Crisis Intake Form" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the form is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. Note that a professional Landing Page will be properly designed toward the end of the project (Sprint 7).
Form Logic Implementation: Transform your static components into a dynamic intake form. Implement the local state management to capture user input for all fields defined in the PM's Data Table.

Client-Side Validation: Code the immediate UI feedback logic (e.g., preventing empty submissions, checking input formats, or ensuring required fields are filled) before the data even hits the database.
Data Integration (The Wiring): Connect your intake form to the Database's POST endpoint. Implement the fetch or axios calls to send the user data to the secure database.

Responsive Feedback & Error Handling: Use the database's response parameters to trigger UI changes. Build the logic for loading states, specific field error messages, and the redirect to the Confirmation screen.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your state management is efficient and your intake form logic is robust.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the flow. Confirm that the intake form correctly handles both a "Happy Path" (success) and "Edge Cases" (validation errors).
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your "Request" body perfectly matches their endpoint's expectations and that you are correctly parsing their "Response" parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Volunteer Dashboard User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, table structures, or navigation hallways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Volunteer Dashboard UI atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 4 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Volunteer Dashboard. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live intake flow and successful navigation to the confirmation screen.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Intake Experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Management Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the next phase of the product’s technical evolution, and External Validation to ground the upcoming Volunteer Management Dashboard in market reality.

You are responsible for ensuring that the management experience is not just a technical dashboard, but a tool that fits the high-speed operational needs of those overseeing mental health support requests.

You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual operational habits of staff, volunteers, and coordinators.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Volunteer Management Dashboard. Clarify management details that will affect the build, such as how volunteers prefer to navigate request lists and detailed case views.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Volunteer Management Dashboard, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the management logic and status transitions are intended to work.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential "Coordinators" or "Volunteers" (e.g., mental health professionals, support staff, or community leaders). Present the high-level concept of the management dashboard to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital management workflow (specifically the data filtering and request processing) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of a coordinator managing incoming support requests.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all management-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of internal users.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding volunteer management dashboards or support software. Ensure the team has access to these benchmarks for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize dashboard features and business logic based on real-world operational urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 5 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the AI Prioritization Agent, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next phase of the project.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Volunteer Management Dashboard.

Sprint 3 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 4.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from data entry to data management, exploring how coordinators and trained volunteers will interact with, process, and oversee the incoming requests for mental health support.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms raw request data into organized, manageable outcomes.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Dashboard Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 3 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.
Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required management views (Filtered List vs. Detailed View) and identify the specific business logic for the Volunteer work process.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Volunteer Management Dashboard card in the “User Stories” Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story (“As a... I want... so that...” ), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Age, Gender, Type of Distress), Data Type, and required User Actions (e.g., Assign, Notify). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific “Done” conditions, such as functional filtering and sorting.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs and detail views. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Team Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Volunteer Management logic based on your Data Specification.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the dashboard.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the management logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 4
Sprint 4 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Management Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you expand the visual ecosystem.

Your mission is to move from the core brand elements of Sprint 2 into a high-fidelity dashboard experience for the Volunteer Management Dashboard.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the layouts for the request lists, detail views, and interactive management elements.

Your goal is to ensure that complex data feels organized, clear, and easy to act upon from any device, maintaining the professional trust established by the brand.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify user priorities, management workflows, and specific data points required for the Volunteer Management Dashboard.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Volunteer Management Dashboard user story is drafted. Review the defined acceptance criteria and data hierarchy to ensure your dashboard design aligns with the administrative workflow.

Dashboard & Detail Design: Create the high-fidelity design for the management interface in Figma. Ensure the layout includes both the request header (for high-level tracking) and the detail items (for granular request information).

Action States & Feedback: Design the interactive elements for the management flow, such as buttons for user actions and visual feedback for status changes. Ensure these states follow the established visual language and color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs for the upcoming module. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the UI components align with the established design system.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final Volunteer Management Dashboard designs to the team. This will serve as the "North Star" for the upcoming development in Sprint 4.
Sprint 5 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the AI Prioritization Agent. This will be your research and design focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Management Logic and Data Retrieval
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the entry point to the Volunteer Management Dashboard.

You are no longer just building pipes for input. You are the Management Engine Specialist.

Your mission is to build the retrieval systems and logic gates that allow coordinators and trained volunteers to oversee, filter, and process incoming requests for mental health support.

By the end of this sprint, your dashboard must be able to serve complex data sets, including both request summaries and detailed case information, and execute status updates based on volunteer actions.

You are responsible for ensuring that the Volunteer Management Dashboard foundation is robust, performant, and ready to support the operational workflow.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 4 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the management build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 4 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Database Migration/Updates: Implement any structural changes or new fields required for tracking volunteer engagement and request management views. Ensure indexes are correctly set for efficient filtering and sorting.

Query Logic Build: Code the retrieval logic to fetch both high-level request summaries and granular case details. Ensure the data is structured to support the filtering and sorting behavior defined in the PM's User Story.

API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for the dashboard and detailed views, alongside the PATCH/PUT endpoints to handle updates on volunteer assignments. Ensure the response body includes the updated state objects for UI responsiveness.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your retrieval logic and volunteer assignment functions. Ensure that unauthorized or invalid state changes are rejected with the correct error codes.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 3 to verify the dashboard endpoints. Confirm that the filters return the correct data and that status updates persist in the database before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the dashboard. Verify that your endpoints are serving the correct JSON structure for the request summaries and case details and responding correctly to user actions.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the AI Prioritization Agent Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the AI Prioritization schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the AI Prioritization Agent. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional dashboard logic and data processing.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Volunteer Management Dashboard.

Cards.Name: Volunteer Management Dashboard UI and Data Interaction
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from data collection to data orchestration. 
 
 You are no longer just building input forms. You are building the Volunteer Management Experience. 
 
 Your mission is to take the dashboard components and navigation pathways from Sprint 3 and wire them into a fully functional interface for coordinators and volunteers to oversee and process incoming requests for mental health support. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, a user should be able to navigate a dynamic list of requests, drill down into detailed case information, and execute response actions through responsive Accept/Decline options. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that complex request data is rendered clearly and that every administrative action provides immediate, reliable feedback.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 4 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the dashboard behavior and action logic match the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 4 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Volunteer Management Dashboard" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the management interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be designed in Sprint 7.

Dashboard Logic Implementation: Transform your static table and list components into a dynamic interface. Implement state management to handle the display of request headers and the "drill-down" logic for detailed case information.
Filtering & Sorting Engine: Code the client-side logic for data organization. Ensure users can seamlessly filter and sort the incoming requests based on the parameters defined in the PM's User Story.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your dashboard to the Backend’s GET endpoints for list/detail views and the PATCH/PUT endpoints for Accept/Decline actions.

Responsive Action Handling: Build the logic for interactive feedback. Ensure that clicking Accept or Decline triggers the correct loading states and updates the UI in real-time based on the server's response.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the management flow. Confirm that the dashboard correctly handles data rendering, filtering, and the successful execution of response actions.

Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your data requirements for the "Request Header + Case Detail" view match their response structure and that action parameters are correctly aligned.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the AI Prioritization User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, map components, or navigation pathways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the AI Prioritization UI components and navigation pathways are built and ready for the Sprint 5 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the AI Prioritization Agent. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live management flow, showing the dashboard navigation and the successful processing of a request.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Volunteer Management experience.

Cards.Name: AI Prioritization and Risk Assessment
Cards.Description:
This week, you explore the requirements for the project’s AI Prioritization Agent.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the AI's evaluation processes for incoming mental health requests, and External Validation to ground the upcoming AI Prioritization Agent in real-world application.

You are responsible for ensuring that the prioritization experience is not just a technical assessment, but a functional tool that fits the real-time operational needs of volunteers responding to urgent cases.

You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual operational habits of volunteers, crisis responders, and mental health professionals.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the AI Prioritization Agent. Clarify data details that will affect the build, such as how urgency scores are calculated and what information volunteers need to respond most urgently.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the AI Prioritization Agent, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the urgency scoring and case flagging are intended to work.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2–3 potential "Volunteers" or "Crisis Responders" (e.g., mental health professionals, volunteer coordinators, or crisis hotline staff). Present the high-level concept of the prioritization interface to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital prioritization workflow aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of a volunteer managing multiple urgent requests during a busy shift.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all mental health-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of the volunteers.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding mental health support systems, AI evaluation methods, or crisis intervention benchmarks. Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize features and scoring logic based on real-world operational urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 6 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the Reporting and Analytics, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next logistical phase of the project.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the AI Prioritization Agent.

Sprint 4 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding the prioritization features in Sprint 5.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from internal data management to urgent response operations, exploring how incoming request data and service integrations will be used to facilitate timely mental health support.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms incoming requests into prioritized cases for the team.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Prioritization Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 4 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required prioritization views (e.g., Urgency Overview vs. Case Details) and identify the specific business logic for dynamic urgency scoring and case flagging.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing AI Prioritization Agent card in the “User Stories” Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a volunteer... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Request Data, Urgency Score, Risk Indicators), Data Type, and required User Actions. 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions relevant to the module’s core logic and technical requirements.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs for request prioritization and volunteer assignment views. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Team Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the AI Prioritization logic and external service interactions.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the prioritization views.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the prioritization logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 5
Sprint 5 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Logistics Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you translate the operational framework into a high-utility interface for the AI Prioritization Agent.

Your mission is to design a triage-focused user interface for the AI Prioritization Agent.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" that defines how the coordinator reviews and prioritizes incoming crisis requests, from urgency scoring to the assignment of appropriate volunteers.

Your goal is to ensure that the interface is glanceable and decision-focused, providing coordinators with the essential information needed to identify high-risk cases and prioritize responses quickly, without unnecessary complexity.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify key indicators of risk, essential data points, and the preferred workflow for evaluating incoming requests. Additionally, clarify how the AI Customer expects the output view to be presented, including how urgency scores, flagged high-risk cases, and recommended volunteer matches should appear in the prioritization interface.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the AI Prioritization Agent user story is drafted. Review the defined urgency scoring criteria, case flagging logic, and data processing requirements to ensure your mobile framework is aligned with the operational plan.

AI Interface Design: Create the high-level interface design for the AI prioritization triage view in Figma.
Design Focus: Establish the visual framework for displaying urgency scores, highlighted high-risk cases, and AI-recommended volunteer matches, enabling coordinators to quickly review and prioritize incoming requests.

Request Action States: Design the interactive elements for the AI Customer’s workflow, such as buttons for case updates and urgency assessments. Ensure these actions provide immediate visual confirmation using the established color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. 
 Synchronization: Confirm the technical approach for integrating data inputs and outputs.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final AI Prioritization Agent designs to the team. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the High-Level Interface and Prioritization layouts 
 (2) Demonstrate the core urgency scoring flow 
 (3) Confirm design handoff for Sprint 5 execution
Sprint 6 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Reporting and Analytics. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: AI Prioritization Logic
Cards.Description:
This week, the AI Prioritization Agent moves into full operational mode.

You are no longer just managing data; you are the AI Specialist.

Your mission is to build the computational logic that evaluates incoming crisis request data and converts it into prioritized triage outputs.

By the end of this sprint, your module must be able to analyze incoming request data and generate real-time urgency scores that populate the AI prioritization interface, allowing coordinators to identify and review high-risk cases before assignment in the Volunteer Management Dashboard.

You are responsible for ensuring that the AI Prioritization Agent foundation is reliable and provides clear, decision-ready prioritization outputs that support fast and effective volunteer response in critical situations.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 5 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the prioritization build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 5 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Data Schema Updates: Implement any structural changes or new fields required for processing incoming requests and urgency scoring. Ensure necessary performance optimizations are implemented for efficient data analysis.

Urgency Scoring Logic Build: Conduct a technical deep-dive into the LLM capabilities and natural language processing techniques to identify critical parameters for risk assessment. Code the logic to analyze incoming requests and assign urgency scores, consulting with the AI Mentor to ensure the integration is secure and performant.
API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for retrieving urgency scores and flagged high-risk cases, alongside the POST endpoints to handle incoming request submissions.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your urgency scoring logic and case flagging functions. Ensure that request analysis and data integration are handled gracefully.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your Mock Data from Sprint 4 to verify the urgency scoring endpoints. Confirm that the scoring logic returns the expected urgency scores and that high-risk cases are flagged correctly.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to wire up the  AI Prioritization Dashboard. Verify that your endpoints are serving the correct data structure for real-time updates and that volunteer recommendations are functional.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Reporting and Analytics User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Reporting and Analytics schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Reporting and Analytics module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional urgency scoring logic.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the AI Prioritization Agent module.

Cards.Name: AI Prioritization Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, the interface is set to enhance mental health support.

Your mission is to build the mobile-first AI Prioritization Agent interface.

You are no longer building for a desktop dashboard; you are building for a volunteer in the field who needs high glanceability and quick interaction.

By the end of this sprint, your interface must be able to render a prioritized list of incoming requests, integrate visual indicators for urgency, and handle real-time updates on case statuses.

You are responsible for ensuring that the prioritization flow is intuitive, responsive, and robust enough to manage critical mental health requests effectively.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 5 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the prioritization behavior matches the volunteer's workflow.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 5 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add an "AI Prioritization" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the prioritization interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be designed in Sprint 7.
Mobile Request Implementation: Transform your request atoms into a dynamic, mobile-optimized list. Implement state management to handle the display of the urgency scores and flagged cases.

Urgency Indicator Integration: Implement visual indicators for urgency as defined in the UI/UX design. Build the logic to receive and render updates dynamically from the Backend API once the requests are processed.

API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your interface to the Backend’s GET endpoints to fetch incoming crisis request data and PATCH/PUT endpoints to update volunteer actions or case status (e.g., "Acknowledged," "Resolved").

Field Action Handling: Build the logic for request status transitions. Ensure that the interface provides clear, immediate feedback when a request is marked as urgent or when a volunteer updates a case.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your mobile responsiveness and state transitions are efficient.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the prioritization flow. Confirm that urgency scores render correctly and that status changes persist through the API.
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that the request data, urgency scores, and dynamic volunteer recommendations in the JSON structure match your interface requirements.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Reporting and Analytics User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, data visualization needs, or navigation hallways for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the Reporting and Analytics UI atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 6 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Reporting and Analytics module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live prioritization flow and mobile field interactions.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the AI Prioritization Agent experience.

Cards.Description:

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand how incoming data is aggregated and processed, and External Validation to ensure the reports provide actual, actionable insights for volunteers, coordinators, and program managers.

You are responsible for ensuring that the analytics experience is not just a collection of charts or tables, but a powerful tool for tracking trends, evaluating response performance, and supporting informed operational decisions.

You will bridge the gap between raw data and the real-world decision-making habits of volunteers, coordinators, and mental health leadership.

Clarify which indicators of risk (e.g., potential self-harm vs. severe distress) and data points carry the most weight for volunteer response and operational prioritization.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the AI Prioritization Agent, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate the operational utility of the prioritization workflow, specifically how the interplay between urgency scores, risk indicators, and volunteer assignments drives effective crisis response.

Strategic Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2–3 volunteer coordinators or crisis response managers. Present the high-level concept of the AI Prioritization dashboard, including urgency scoring and flagged case views, to determine if these analytics align with their operational needs and decision-making requirements.

Data-Driven Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed reporting format (tabular vs. graphical) fits the existing "on-the-ground" behavior of Volunteers who currently use spreadsheets or manual logs for data analysis.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all insights related to AI prioritization and volunteer response. Ensure you are managing the stakeholder list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding operational needs and risk assessment preferences for crisis management.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant research on mental health support workflows, volunteer response benchmarks, or crisis prioritization best practices. Ensure the team has access to these references for guidance and design inspiration.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Analytics Experience

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" that defines how a Program Coordinator visualizes impact, from high-level daily or weekly request summaries to detailed historical logs.

Your goal is to ensure that complex mental health data feels organized and actionable, transforming raw intake and volunteer response records into a clear narrative of service impact without overwhelming the user.

 You are responsible for ensuring that complex datasets are presented with high glanceability and that the filtering logic is seamless, allowing managers to extract the specific insights they need to drive the organization forward.
